title: TeamPCP Trojans LiteLLM PyPI Package in Multi-Stage Supply Chain Attack: Malicious Versions 1.82.7–1.82.8 Deploy Credential Stealer, Kubernetes Lateral Movement Toolkit, and Persistent Backdoor via Python .pth Auto-Execution — 3.4 Million Daily Downloads Exposed for 3 Hours
affected_products: Any environment that ran pip install or updated litellm between 10:39–13:40 UTC on March 24, 2026

sources.name: LiteLLM Official Security Update — Suspected Supply Chain Incident
Datadog Security Labs — LiteLLM Compromised on PyPI: Tracing the March 2026 TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign

Arctic Wolf — TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack Campaign Targets Trivy, Checkmarx KICS, and LiteLLM
Snyk — How a Poisoned Security Scanner Became the Key to Backdooring LiteLLM

timeline.event: TeamPCP compromises the aquasecurity/trivy-action GitHub Action by injecting malicious code that exfiltrates CI/CD environment secrets from repositories using the action in their workflows

Sysdig observes identical credential stealer targeting Aqua Security's Trivy vulnerability scanner and its associated GitHub Actions; Checkmarx KICS GitHub Action also compromised by TeamPCP in parallel track

TeamPCP publishes litellm 1.82.7 to PyPI at 10:39 UTC using stolen PYPI_PUBLISH token exfiltrated from LiteLLM's CI/CD pipeline via the compromised Trivy action; version 1.82.8 follows at 10:52 UTC with an upgraded .pth-based payload
PyPI safety team quarantines litellm versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 approximately 3 hours after publication at ~13:40 UTC; LiteLLM maintainers publish clean version 1.82.9

Multiple security firms (Datadog, Wiz, Snyk, Sonatype, Arctic Wolf) publish coordinated analysis attributing the attack to TeamPCP and detailing the full kill chain from Trivy compromise to PyPI credential theft to malicious package deployment

Investigation ongoing into downstream impact; organizations urged to audit CI/CD pipelines for compromised Trivy and KICS actions and rotate all credentials on systems that installed affected litellm versions

simple_summary.what_happened: A threat actor known as TeamPCP executed a sophisticated multi-stage supply chain attack that culminated in the compromise of LiteLLM, one of the most popular Python AI libraries with 3.4 million daily downloads on PyPI. The attackers first poisoned the Trivy security scanner GitHub Action — a tool ironically used to check for vulnerabilities in CI/CD pipelines. When LiteLLM's automated build pipeline ran the compromised Trivy action, it unknowingly handed over its PyPI publishing credentials to the attackers. TeamPCP then used those stolen credentials to publish two malicious versions of litellm (1.82.7 and 1.82.8) directly to PyPI. The backdoored packages contained a credential stealer that harvested SSH keys, cloud API keys, Kubernetes secrets, cryptocurrency wallet files, and environment variables from every system that installed them. The malicious versions were live on PyPI for approximately 3 hours before being quarantined.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any developer, CI/CD pipeline, or production system that installed or upgraded to litellm versions 1.82.7 or 1.82.8 between 10:39 UTC and approximately 13:40 UTC on March 24, 2026. LiteLLM is widely used across the AI and machine learning industry as a unified interface for calling over 100 LLM APIs including OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, and AWS Bedrock. Organizations running automated pip install or pip install --upgrade commands in CI/CD pipelines, Docker builds, or scheduled dependency updates during this window are at highest risk. The broader TeamPCP campaign also affects users of the compromised Trivy GitHub Action and Checkmarx KICS GitHub Action.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: TeamPCP compromises the aquasecurity/trivy-action GitHub Action by injecting malicious code that silently exfiltrates environment variables and CI/CD secrets (including PyPI tokens, NPM tokens, and cloud credentials) from any repository that uses the action in its workflow. Step 2: When LiteLLM's CI/CD pipeline runs its regular security scan using the compromised Trivy action, the malicious code captures the PYPI_PUBLISH token from the GitHub Actions runner environment and sends it to the attacker's command and control server. Step 3: Using the stolen PyPI credentials, TeamPCP publishes litellm version 1.82.7 with a base64-encoded payload injected into litellm/proxy/proxy_server.py that executes when the proxy module is imported, followed by version 1.82.8 which adds a litellm_init.pth file to site-packages that executes on every Python interpreter startup — even without importing litellm. Step 4: The malicious payload runs a three-stage attack: first, a credential harvester sweeps SSH keys (~/.ssh/), cloud credentials (~/.aws/, ~/.azure/, ~/.gcp/), Kubernetes configs (~/.kube/config), cryptocurrency wallets, and all .env files; second, a Kubernetes lateral movement toolkit deploys privileged pods to every node in accessible clusters; third, a persistent systemd backdoor service (sysmon.service) is installed that polls checkmarx.zone/raw for additional payloads. Step 5: All harvested credentials are encrypted with AES-256 (key wrapped with an embedded RSA public key) and exfiltrated via HTTPS POST to attacker-controlled domains: checkmarx[.]zone for version 1.82.7 and models[.]litellm[.]cloud for version 1.82.8.
what_you_should_do: Immediately check if litellm 1.82.7 or 1.82.8 was installed in any environment between 10:39–13:40 UTC on March 24, 2026 by running: pip show litellm and checking pip download logs, Docker build logs, and CI/CD pipeline artifacts

If either compromised version was installed, treat the entire host as compromised — rotate ALL credentials including AWS keys, Azure tokens, GCP service accounts, SSH keys, database passwords, API keys, Kubernetes secrets, and any secrets stored in .env files

Search for the persistence mechanism: check for sysmon.service in systemd units (systemctl list-units | grep sysmon) and inspect /tmp/pglog for attacker-delivered binaries; check site-packages for litellm_init.pth files

Audit your CI/CD pipelines for use of aquasecurity/trivy-action and Checkmarx/kics-github-action — pin these to known-good commit SHAs rather than version tags, and rotate any secrets that were exposed in workflows using these actions
Upgrade to litellm 1.82.9 or later on clean systems only — do not simply upgrade on potentially compromised hosts as the systemd backdoor persists independently of the Python package
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This attack represents a new evolution in software supply chain warfare — the attackers deliberately compromised security tools (Trivy, a vulnerability scanner) to gain access to the build pipelines of high-value downstream targets. LiteLLM's 3.4 million daily downloads and central role as an LLM API gateway means the blast radius includes AI startups, Fortune 500 companies, and cloud infrastructure running AI workloads. The .pth file technique in version 1.82.8 is particularly dangerous because it executes on every Python process startup, not just when litellm is imported — meaning even running pip install on the same system would trigger the malware. The credential harvesting targets the exact secrets needed for cloud infrastructure takeover: AWS keys, Kubernetes configs, and SSH keys. The Kubernetes lateral movement component can propagate across entire clusters within minutes. Although the malicious versions were only live for 3 hours, automated CI/CD pipelines that pull latest versions would have been compromised silently.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
tactics.description: TeamPCP compromised the Trivy GitHub Action to steal CI/CD credentials, then used those credentials to publish malicious versions of the litellm Python package to PyPI, poisoning the software supply chain at the package repository level

The malicious litellm package uses Python's .pth file mechanism (litellm_init.pth) to achieve automatic code execution on every Python interpreter startup, and source-level injection in proxy_server.py for import-triggered execution

tactics.technique_name: Create or Modify System Process: Systemd Service
tactics.description: The payload installs a persistent systemd service named sysmon.service that continuously polls the attacker's C2 server at checkmarx.zone/raw for additional payloads, surviving package removal or system restarts

The credential harvester systematically searches for and exfiltrates SSH keys, cloud provider credentials, Kubernetes configs, cryptocurrency wallets, and environment variable files from the compromised system

The Kubernetes lateral movement toolkit uses stolen kubeconfig credentials to deploy privileged containers across all nodes in accessible Kubernetes clusters, expanding the attacker's foothold across the entire container infrastructure
Harvested credentials are encrypted with AES-256 (RSA-wrapped key) and exfiltrated via HTTPS POST requests to attacker-controlled domains checkmarx.zone and models.litellm.cloud

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying the malicious litellm package artifacts (.pth files, modified proxy_server.py), persistence mechanisms (sysmon.service systemd unit), credential access patterns (mass file reads across SSH/cloud/K8s credential directories), and network indicators (outbound connections to TeamPCP C2 domains). Organizations should prioritize checking CI/CD pipeline logs for installations during the 3-hour exposure window.

log_sources.source: Package Manager Logs (pip, CI/CD build logs)
log_sources.description: Primary detection source — check pip install logs, Docker build history, and CI/CD pipeline artifacts for any installation of litellm 1.82.7 or 1.82.8 between 10:39–13:40 UTC on March 24, 2026
Monitor for creation of litellm_init.pth in Python site-packages directories, modification of litellm/proxy/proxy_server.py with base64-encoded payloads, and creation of sysmon.service systemd units
Detect outbound HTTPS connections to checkmarx.zone and models.litellm.cloud — these are the primary exfiltration and C2 endpoints used by the TeamPCP payload
Monitor for unauthorized pod creation with privileged security contexts across cluster nodes — the lateral movement toolkit deploys privileged pods to expand the attacker's foothold

what_to_look_for.indicator: Presence of litellm_init.pth in any Python site-packages directory
what_to_look_for.description: The .pth file is the primary persistence and execution mechanism in version 1.82.8 — it triggers malicious code execution on every Python interpreter startup regardless of imports

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound HTTPS connections to checkmarx.zone or models.litellm.cloud
what_to_look_for.description: These are the attacker-controlled C2 and exfiltration domains — checkmarx.zone is used by version 1.82.7 for both data exfiltration and payload delivery; models.litellm.cloud is used by version 1.82.8

what_to_look_for.indicator: Systemd service named sysmon.service polling external URLs
what_to_look_for.description: The persistent backdoor installs as sysmon.service and continuously polls https://checkmarx.zone/raw for additional payloads, downloading them to /tmp/pglog for execution

what_to_look_for.indicator: Mass file access across credential directories (~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.azure, ~/.kube)
what_to_look_for.description: The credential harvester reads SSH keys, cloud provider credentials, and Kubernetes configs in rapid succession — look for a single process accessing multiple credential directories within seconds
what_to_look_for.log_field: process_name='python*' AND file_access_path matches '~/.ssh/*|~/.aws/*|~/.azure/*|~/.kube/*' within 60 seconds

what_to_look_for.indicator: Privileged pod creation across multiple Kubernetes nodes from unexpected source
what_to_look_for.description: The K8s lateral movement toolkit deploys privileged pods to every node — unusual pod creation patterns with privileged security contexts should trigger immediate investigation

immediate_actions.action: Determine if compromised litellm versions were installed
immediate_actions.description: Search all development machines, CI/CD pipelines, Docker images built on March 24, and production servers for litellm 1.82.7 or 1.82.8. Check pip logs, requirements.txt lock files, and container image layers for evidence of these versions being pulled during the 10:39–13:40 UTC exposure window.
immediate_actions.command: pip show litellm 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'Version: 1.82.[78]'; find / -name 'litellm_init.pth' 2>/dev/null; systemctl status sysmon.service 2>/dev/null

immediate_actions.action: Rotate ALL credentials on affected systems immediately
immediate_actions.description: If either compromised version was installed, assume complete credential compromise. Rotate AWS access keys, Azure service principal secrets, GCP service account keys, SSH keypairs, Kubernetes service account tokens, database passwords, and all secrets stored in .env files. Revoke and regenerate all API keys for LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.).

immediate_actions.action: Remove persistence mechanisms and re-image compromised hosts
immediate_actions.description: The systemd backdoor (sysmon.service) persists independently of the Python package. Simply upgrading litellm is insufficient — search for and remove sysmon.service, check /tmp/pglog for downloaded payloads, and remove litellm_init.pth files. For production systems, re-imaging is strongly recommended over manual cleanup.
immediate_actions.command: systemctl stop sysmon.service 2>/dev/null; systemctl disable sysmon.service 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/pglog; find / -name 'litellm_init.pth' -delete 2>/dev/null

immediate_actions.action: Audit CI/CD pipelines for compromised GitHub Actions
immediate_actions.description: Check all repositories for use of aquasecurity/trivy-action and Checkmarx/kics-github-action. Pin these to verified commit SHAs rather than mutable version tags. Review GitHub Actions workflow run logs from March 10 onwards for signs of secret exfiltration. Rotate all CI/CD secrets that may have been exposed.

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade to clean litellm version on verified-clean systems
immediate_actions.description: After confirming systems are not compromised (or after re-imaging), install litellm 1.82.9 or later. Enable pip hash checking (--require-hashes) and consider using a private PyPI mirror with package verification to prevent future supply chain attacks.
